issue_title: Explore going the route of using Clients ?
issue_body:
After our last talk, there have been quite a few different ideas tossed around to make it clear and obvious which credentials and project IDs are in use during a particular API call, some of those have been....

1. Changing the default values globally
1. Making "Connections" a context manager (`with connection: # do something`)
1. Creating the concept of a client

We do (1), are talking about doing (2), while the others tend to do (3) -- and comparing the code, I think (3) is the nicest. 

gcloud-node:

```javascript
var gcloud = require('gcloud');
var project1_storage = gcloud.storage({projectId: 'project1', keyFilename: '/path/to/key'});
var project2_storage_auto = gcloud.storage;

# Do things with the two "clients"...
bucket1 = project1_storage.get_bucket('bucket-1')
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.get_bucket('bucket2')
```

gcloud-ruby

```ruby
require 'gcloud/storage'
project1_storage = Gcloud.storage "project-id-1" "/path/to/key"
project2_storage_auto = Gcloud.storage  # Magically figure out the project ID and credentials

# Do things with the two "clients"...
bucket1 = project1_storage.find_bucket "bucket-1"
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.find_bucket "bucket-2"
```

gcloud-python

```python
from gcloud import storage
from gcloud.credentials import get_for_service_account_json

# Create two different credentials.
credentials1 = get_for_service_account_json('key1.json')
credentials2 = get_for_service_account_json('key2.json')

# Create two different connections.
connection1 = storage.Connection(credentials=credentials1)
connection2 = storage.Connection(credentials=credentials2)

# Get two different buckets
bucket1 = storage.get_bucket('bucket-1', project='project1', connection=connection1)
bucket2 = storage.get_bucket('bucket-2', project='project2', connection=connection2)
```

gcloud-python if we followed the client pattern:

```python
from gcloud import storage

project1_storage = storage.Client('project1', '/path/to/key')
project2_storage_auto = storage.Client()

# Do things with the two "clients"...
bucket1 = project1_storage.get_bucket('bucket-1')
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.get_bucket('bucket-2')
```

/cc @dhermes @tseaver

issue_title: Controller keeps rescheduling itself.
issue_body:
In python the controller callback (Used to update the UI) continuously runs. See:

https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1414

https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/python/src/mapreduce/handlers.py#L1124

The problem is that this can continue even after the job has finished. 
An easy patch is to get more aggressive about stopping or backing off the controller. However the preferred way, would be to move towards the way the Java impl works, by having the controller only used once to synchronize the end of the shard's life, and managing the UI elsewhere.

issue_title: Installing with pip -t broken
issue_body:
1. OS type and version
`Linux localhost.localdomain 4.11.11-300.fc26.x86_64 #1 SMP Mon Jul 17 16:32:11 UTC 2017 x86_64 x86_64 x86_64 GNU/Linux`

Rest of the questions are context sensitive.

If I install google-cloud in someEnv like so...

```bash

[stephen@localhost ~]$ python2.7 -m pip --version
pip 9.0.1 from /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages (python 2.7)

[stephen@localhost ~]$ virtualenv someEnv
New python executable in /home/stephen/someEnv/bin/python2.7
Also creating executable in /home/stephen/someEnv/bin/python
Installing setuptools, pip, wheel...done.

[stephen@localhost ~]$ source someEnv/bin/activate
(someEnv) [stephen@localhost ~]$ python2.7 -m pip install google-cloud --upgrade
# ...snip...
(someEnv) [stephen@localhost ~]$ ls someEnv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google
api  auth  cloud  gapic  gax  iam  logging  longrunning  oauth2  protobuf  resumable_media  rpc  type

```
Those folders look good, right?

Now, lets say I want to use `cloud` with AppEngine Standard. You would normally use `-t some_target` with pip and vendor `some_target` in your appengine_config.py, right?

```bash
[stephen@localhost ~]$ mkdir myAppEngineProject
[stephen@localhost ~]$ python2.7 -m pip install google-cloud -t myAppEngineProject/lib --upgrade
# ...snip...
[stephen@localhost ~]$ ls myAppEngineProject/lib/google
protobuf
```

So only protobuf makes it through. Without the `--upgrade` switch, this happens:

```bash

[stephen@localhost ~]$ rm myAppEngineProject/lib/ -rf
[stephen@localhost ~]$ python2.7 -m pip install google-cloud -t myAppEngineProject/lib
# ...snip...
Target directory /home/stephen/myAppEngineProject/lib/google already exists. Specify --upgrade to force replacement.
[stephen@localhost ~]$ ls myAppEngineProject/lib/google
api  auth  cloud  gapic  gax  iam  logging  longrunning  oauth2  resumable_media  rpc  type

```
So now `protobuf ` is missing and pip just wants to wipe the whole google dir when upgrading, even from a fresh install.

This is also an issue with just wanting to install `google-cloud-pubsub`, guess other sub-projects might be affected.

issue_title: BigQuery: no public method to retry/resubmit an existing job
issue_body:
There is currently no public method to resubmit an existing job for instance when a transient error
like 403 **rateLimitExceeded** is returned.

As a workaround, we currently use the following, which works for `query` and `load` : 

    bq = bigquery.Client()    
    job_ref = bq.get_job('jobid_that_failed_because_of_ratelimit')._build_resource()
    job_ref['jobReference']['jobId'] = str(uuid4())

    # clear properties set by job response
     if 'query' in job_ref['configuration']:
                 del (job_ref['configuration']['query']['destinationTable'])

      new_job = bq.job_from_resource(job_ref)
      new_job._begin()

Request is to have a method that works for any job type and is included in core package - as above private methods may change

issue_title: Replace `pytest_run.py` use of `--resultlog` with `--junitxml`.
issue_body:
In order to upgrade pytest, coverage et. al. we need to replace the current use of `--resultlog` with something else since its deprecated and slated for removal.  The py.test 3.0.2 output is:
```
...
platform linux2 -- Python 2.7.12, pytest-3.0.2, py-1.4.31, pluggy-0.3.1
rootdir: /, inifile: 
plugins: timeout-1.0.0, cov-2.3.1
collected 12 items
...
============= pytest-warning summary =============
...
WC1 None --result-log is deprecated and scheduled for removal in pytest 4.0
...
```

We already parse junit xml format in `junit_run.py` to find the same data, and since `py.test` and `pytest_run.py` already support this format, it seems reasonable to switch to parsing it.

issue_title: cloudformation deploy CLI exits with return code 255 if stack exists
issue_body:
I'm trying to deploy AWS SAM in a CI/CD pipeline. I'm calling the "deploy" command from an ansible shell which then fails if the stack exists

If "aws cloudformation deploy" is run on an existing stack (e.g. re-run) it outputs:

> No changes to deploy. Stack <stack-name> is up to date


And exits with error code 255.

Based on
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/topic/return-codes.html
I'd suggest this is not really a "Command failed" error.

Better would be to return 0, or another value (!=0 and != 255) to indicate no-op.

These are all covered in #248 but I am between a rock and a hard place. My go live date is July and I have to make a decision. Do I wait for the items in #248 and hope that they make it in or do I just assume these features won't make it in time. This will force me to (painfully) write hacks in my code to get everything to work.

I tried to get a sense of timeline from @jfuss comment on Jan 8 when he said "All of these (in the issue) are being prioritize with high priority in the team and are going to be the first set of things we will address this year."

Can you please provide either an ETA or tell us your release plan? Will it be one large release or will it be minor releases? Any insight will be appreciated.

Again keep up the great work.

aldryn-categories
aldryn-people
aldryn-reversion
djangocms-text-ckeditor
easy_thumbnails
django-filer
django-parler
django-reversion
taggit
```

issue_title: Do we need to deal w/ 0-based and 1-based matrices?
issue_body:
Cube, Transcad, and Emme all use zone numbers that are 1-based, which is a huge pain in the ass.

How do we want to deal with this?  A flag? Ignore it and leave it to the user?

issue_title: Includes not turned in to Services/Operations
issue_body:
I'm using a WSDL where operations are defined in an include as follows:
```
<wsdl:types>
    <xsd:schema targetNamespace="http://mangled.com" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">
        <xsd:include schemaLocation="xsd/FindServiceProviderLocationIdRequest.xsd" />
    </xsd:schema>
</wsdl:types>
```

These includes are then not turned into operations with the error `RuntimeError: Operation FindServiceProviderLocationIdRequest not found in WSDL: Service/Port Type: (u'MANGLED', u'MANGLED')`

The XML files seemed to be imported correctly, they're just not added to the services list.

issue_title: Complex Ruge-Stuben Support
issue_body:
Currently, the classical RS interpolation and associated C++ routines, rs_direct_interpolation_pass1, rs_direct_interpolation_pass2, do not support complex arithmetic. Also, the interpolation formula used right now is not known to be compatible with the complex case. Scott MacLachlan recently had a paper with a formula for classical AMG type interpolation in the complex case.

Migrated from http://code.google.com/p/pyamg/issues/detail?id=61

earlier comments

jacob.b....@gmail.com said, at 2010-10-10T04:52:01.000Z:

My motivation for this change isn't that great. Its bascially implementing the algorithm from a MacLachlan and Oosterlee paper about AMG for complex matrices.

issue_title: Add support for .fai FASTA index files
issue_body: We need a way to pull subjects out of a large FASTA file (ideally files, but let's just do one file for now). The FASTA index (fai) files that samtools makes give one way to do this. It's not optimal in some ways, but it's easy to implement, so let's try that. A better option, which allows the FASTA to remain compressed, would be the `BGZF` format. It's not simple to see how to work all these possible options into one FASTA reads class. For now do the simplest thing and keep moving on the actual work.

issue_title: exabgp crashes with permission denied error.
IBGP Peering on Loopback addresses?
issue_body:
##### ISSUE TYPE
How to write EXABGP config file if the nodes are using ibgp?
##### OS
Ubuntu 14

##### SUMMARY

```
|--------------| 2.0.19.1/24       2.0.19.2/24       |-----------------|
|    Node A    |------------------------------------ |    Node B       |
| lo:1.0.1.1   |                                     |                  |
|--------------|                                      |-----------------|

```
Consider Node A and Node B directly connected with the ip addresses shown above. If we have Node A in Quagga with the configuration as:
```

router bgp 200 
 bgp router-id 172.0.2.1
 network 2.102.0.0/24
 neighbor 2.0.19.2 remote-as 700
 neighbor 2.0.19.2 update-source lo
```

Node B Quagga:

```
router bgp 700
 bgp router-id 2.45.55.5

issue_title: Withdrawal of flow spec announce on ExaBGP daemon interruption
issue_body:
Hello, Tomas!

I have implemented very draft integration of netmap-ipfw and flow spec with huge help from ExaBGP!

Now I could add filtering rules from flow spec announces https://github.com/FastVPSEestiOu/fastnetmon/blob/master/src/scripts/firewall_queue.py :)

But when I interrupt ExaBGP which announces this flows I expected withdrawal of announces because I want to remove they from firewall tables. But there are no actions in update log about it after CTRL+C.

Maybe you can add this feature for explicit switching off announced flows?

Additionally, will be fine to get way to notify my tool about "wasted flows", i.e. flows from peers which go away.

issue_title: docker-py version "1.4.0-dev" network_mode bug with docker 1.7.1
issue_body:
With the introduction of [this code](https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blame/master/docker/utils/utils.py#L432) to cater for docker 1.8's `network_mode` option of `default`, it now breaks the integration tests if you are on docker 1.7 with:

`NotFound: 404 Client Error: Not Found ("Cannot start container 1a0b16410775af68692e174462b94c8ebd9e79be31da27d3b870b63dd83d2625: error locating network with name default: network default not found")`

I propose that we add in a `compare_version` around the setting of the `network_mode` and if the API version < 1.20 we don't set it to `default`, so that we are backwards compatible for docker 1.7.

issue_title: The behaviour of ``Client.wait()`` needs better documentation when ``timeout`` is used.
issue_body:
Based on reading the documentation for [``Client.wait()``](http://docker-py.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/#wait) I expected ``-1`` to be returned if the specified ``timeout`` was hit. Instead an exception (``requests.exceptions.ReadTimeout``) seems to be hit in different ways internally depending on whether I'm using Python2 or Python3.

Here is an example

```python
import docker

def main():
  client = docker.Client()

  container = client.create_container(
    image='ubuntu:14.04',
    command=['sleep', '100']
    )

  try:
    client.start(container=container['Id'])
    # Now wait
    exitCode = client.wait(container=container['Id'], timeout=5)
    if exitCode == -1:
      print("Timeout??")
  finally:
    client.remove_container(container=container['Id'], force=True)

if __name__ == '__main__':
  main()
```

**Thoughts**

* If returning ``-1`` is not for when the ``timeout`` is hit, when will it be returned?

issue_title: [1.7] regression in .dockerignore handling
issue_body:
If the `Dockerfile` is being ignored by a path in the `.dockerignore` file, it is incorrectly being removed from the context. There is a special case handling when the file is being excluded directly, but it should also apply when there is a path which includes the `Dockerfile`.

Possibly caused by #863

```
docker-py version: 1.7.0

$ tree -a
.
├── Docker
│   ├── dc.yml
│   └── Dockerfile.debug
└── .dockerignore

1 directory, 3 files

$ cat Docker/Dockerfile.debug

FROM alpine:3.3
RUN  echo building
CMD  echo run

$ cat Docker/dc.yml

app:
  build: ..
  dockerfile: Docker/Dockerfile.debug

$ cat .dockerignore
Docker/

docker-compose -f Docker/dc.yml build
ERROR: Cannot locate specified Dockerfile: Docker/Dockerfile.debug
```

issue_title: DOC/API: Add all kwargs to user-facing linking functions?
issue_body:
Since the refactoring of the linking code for `find_link`, the various linking functions have mostly relied on `**kwargs`, and those options are only fully documented for `link()`; the other docstrings refer the user to the `link()` documentation.

This is wonderful from the maintainer's point of view. However I think there is a cost to the user, at least to users like me. Various autocompleters (especially IPython) and linters use the explicit kwarg declarations to provide a great convenience. And IPython's instant documentation is less useful when the docstring is incomplete. This is one maddening (yet forgivable! Sorry @tacaswell ) thing about matplotlib.

What are your opinions about this? I am willing to do the work of re-adding all the declarations and/or docstrings for the v0.4 release. I don't think that maintaining that state in the v0.2 and v0.3 series was particularly onerous, but it was hardly automatic and it's worth discussing.

issue_title: In-place upgrade instructions should include deisctl
issue_body:
Our in-place upgrade instructions work, but we forgot `/etc/deis-release` and `deisctl`; both will stay at the previous release. It's a bit tedious but doing something like this on each node is a workaround for now:
```console
$ export DEIS_RELEASE=v1.0.0
$ echo $DEIS_RELEASE > /etc/deis-release
$ curl -sSL http://deis.io/deisctl/install.sh | sudo sh -s $DEIS_RELEASE
$ sudo deisctl refresh-units -t $DEIS_RELEASE -p /var/lib/deis/units
```

issue_title: Feature: Allow System & Application monitoring based on Graphite
issue_body:
Warning: I have the feeling there should already be a bug report about this (maybe even commented on by me?) but I couldn't find it (I only looked at open bug reports though...)

As a Deis System Administrator I want to see

* Global (VM) Performance statistics over time (memory, CPU Utilization, Network Usage, (free) disk space). Normally this can be done by any networking system (zabbix), but "auto" configuration should be central (eg adding a node should automatically use this system).
Bonus points if I can add alerts that trigger on certain tresholds..

As a Deis user
* Per container metrics. This includes Memory Usage, CPU Usage, Network Usage etc per container & also averages per container type (web, worker, cmd, ...).
* Per web metrics. On top of that for the web type containers I also want metrics on requests (number of requests, response times, number of failed requests)...
This is similar to what Heroku provides. (https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/metrics)
* As bonus points I should be able to use the same toolkit for custom metrics (number of logins, ...)

I think graphite together with collectd / statsd and others will be a perfect method to implement this.

Obviously all of this would probably be implemented as optional components that can just run onto the coreos cluster...


Note: I Understand it is not in Deis' scope to write a new monitoring tool that implements storing metrics, triggers etc... I merely propose using existing tools, whereby I think Graphite based stack might be a good start (althoug something else can probably work as well).


If there is any intrest in this I might have a look at it mystelf to build out a basic platform...

issue_body:
### What is wrong?

It's expected that we hit this method quite often:

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/46f956ea048eafed6606f59a465af375f1be7a9a/trinity/tx_pool/validators.py#L47-L56

As innocent as it seems on first glance, this is hitting the `ChainProxy` which means there's inter-process communication involved, making this a good target for some caching.

### How can it be fixed

User a time based caching. Max life time of cached entry should be 5 minutes. Maybe we could use a convenience decorator such as [`@cachetools.func.ttl_cache`](http://cachetools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#cachetools.func.ttl_cache).

issue_title: Add support for tuple types
Websocket provider
issue_body:
I think it's time we have a talk about adding a websocket provider.
- web3.js v.1.0 deprecated `HttpProvider` in favor of `WebsocketProvider`
- infura [added](https://github.com/INFURA/infura/issues/29) public websocket endpoints
- there is an excellent [websockets](https://websockets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) library
- websockets give 4-8x better performance compared to HTTP/IPC from my tests

downsides:
- lots of code to rewrite
- supporting both sync and async code is hard

issue_title: Package versions with features are not correctly resolved
issue_body:
Initially, I thought this was conda's intended behavior, but now I suspect it is a bug. Suppose I built packages 
```
track_vc11-1.0-0.tar.bz2
foo-1.0-0.tar.bz2
foo-1.0-vc11.tar.bz2
foo-2.0-0.tar.bz2
use_foo-1.0-vc11.tar.bz2
```
from the following `meta.yaml` files:
```yaml
package:
  name:  track_vc11
  version: "1.0"
build:
  features:
    - vc11
  track_features:
    - vc11
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  foo
  version: "1.0"
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  foo
  version: "1.0"
build:
  string: vc11
  features:
    - vc11
requirements:
  build:
    - track_vc11
  run:
    - track_vc11
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  foo
  version: "2.0"
```
```yaml
package:
  name:  use_foo
  version: "1.0"
build:
  string: vc11
  features:
    - vc11
requirements:
  build:
    - track_vc11
    - foo
  run:
    - track_vc11
    - foo
```
Now I create an environment where `vc11` is being tracked:
```
conda create --use-local -n vc11 track_vc11
activate vc11
```
Within this environment, I install `use_foo` and get:
```
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:

    foo:     2.0-0
    use_foo: 1.0-vc11 [vc11]
```
However, I expected the output to be
```
    foo:     1.0-vc11 [vc11]
    use_foo: 1.0-vc11 [vc11]
```
because only `foo-1.0-vc11.tar.bz2` has the required feature `vc11`. I digged a bit into the code and found the suspicious statement ([resolve.py#477](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L477))
```python
            dists = self.get_dists(specs, max_only=True)
```
When I changed this into
```python
            dists = self.get_dists(specs, max_only=False)
```
I got the desired output.

issue_title: add prioritize_channels to conda.exports
tar.gz automatically uncompressed by conda -> conda-build md5 check KO
issue_body:
_From @afeblot on October 25, 2016 20:36_

With conda 4.2.11 (and 4.2.10) and conda build 2.0.7 (and 2.0.6), 

Building the zlib recipe from conda-recipe fails with:

MD5 mismatch: '1142191120b845f4ed8c8c17455420ac' != '44d667c142d7cda120332623eab69f40'

It seems conda:fetch.py:download() (thanks to requests) is automatically gunzipping the archive, and checks the md5 of the 2.7 uncompressed .tar (even though the created file is still named .tar.gz), whereas the md5 specified in the recipe is obviously the one of the original compressed archive.

I don't know which behavior is wanted for download(), but it's currently not consistent with conda-build.


_Copied from original issue: conda/conda-build#1483_

issue_title: Problem: when building a package the http auth credentials are lost in 4.4.2
issue_body:
This is most likely introduced by 4.4.2 - has not existed before!

We are using channels that have http auth enabled. Our channel urls look like this: `https://user:password@host.com/path/to/channel/`. Conda in all versions works fine with this. After updating to conda 4.4.2, our conda-build calls fail with:

```
conda.exceptions.CondaHTTPError: HTTP 401 UNAUTHORIZED for url https://host.com/path/to/channel/
```

Downgrading to 4.4.1 resolves the problem. We saw this error on all platforms (win/linux).

There are two interesting observations:
- conda still works with auth, just the conda-build process does not
- the error message indicates, that the credentials get lost. When a _wrong_ password is specified, then the credentials are usually printed alongside: `conda.exceptions.CondaHTTPError: HTTP 401 UNAUTHORIZED for url https://user:wrong_password@host.com/path/to/channel/`. That leaves the impression that the auth info get lost/dropped somewhere

@kalefranz @msarahan  I'm not 100% sure whether this is a conda or conda-build bug - but i think its introduced by conda 4.4.2...

    core.repositoryformatversion=0

    remote.origin.fetch=+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*

    [core]

The OSVVM release distributed with VUnit is now about 18 months old, and pre-dates full compatibilty with ghdl (and indeed the VHDL-2008 standard) specifically regarding impure operations within pure functions.

This presents an unnecessary obstacle to using VUnit with OSVVM and ghdl - a fully open source toolchain.

The issue is easily resolved by updating to OSVVM 2016.1 (released Feb 20,2016)

At the moment the flag --host only supports a single host meaning you have point curator at a single node in the cluster. However if that host goes down for whatever reason then you cant manage your indices.

issue_title: Curator snapshot action timeout override
issue_body:
As discuses with @untergeek on [discuss.elastic.co](https://discuss.elastic.co/t/curator-snapshot-action-timeout-override/58362) automatic `timeout_override` for actions like **snapshot** and **forcemerge** is not working in latest version of curator. This should be mentioned somewhere ideally in documentation found [here](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/configfile.html#timeout).

This is quote of @untergeek reply to my question on discuss.elastic.co relevant to this issue:

> You need to manually change the `timeout_override` for these actions for now. I initially had the feature in place, but removed it when I added `timeout_override`.
>But don't despair, the automatic, longer `timeout_override` for these actions will be fixed in 4.1.0 as a part of the schema validation feature being worked on right now.

issue_title: Transactional behavior for template deployments
issue_body:
This issue has been brought up internally a few times and again in issue #3574.

If you have an ARM template deployment (such as VM create) and it fails midway through, several resources may have already been created for you. In many cases, this is what you want--you'd tweak your parameters, redeploy the template and achieve your desired end-state without needing to delete those resources or recreate them.

However, it is certainly reasonable that a user would want to have some kind of transactional behavior for a command where, if the command fails, the user can assume they were in the same state they were before the ran the command.

This would be easy for template-based creates. We know what resources we plan to create. We could include a `--transaction` flag (or something) to signify that if any of the resources fail to deploy, clean up anything that was created.

It might get a little trickier for the generic `group deployment create` command, but still, conceptually, a `--transaction` flag makes sense.

Thoughts?

issue_title: vm create --generate-ssh-keys is unsafe
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**

When running `vm create --generate-ssh-keys`, the logic of whether to overwrite keys is not as safe as used elsewhere in the product. This has caused us some turbulence with our CI builds, as we do include a private key but not a public key in our build servers, and running with `--generate-ssh-keys` causes our keys to get wiped out.

Ideally, the same logic from batchai custom would be implemented for `vm create` so a warning is shown when a key is present.

- batchai (safe): https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/5a11b04d09aa410be7103577a6bd97c4918b82e4/src/command_modules/azure-cli-batchai/azure/cli/command_modules/batchai/custom.py#L604
- vm create (unsafe): https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/008b1991651aa7e36e0ce6174048bcc406e0e52c/src/command_modules/azure-cli-vm/azure/cli/command_modules/vm/_validators.py#L863


**To Reproduce**
Have `~/.ssh/id_rsa` but not `~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub` in your home folder
Run `vm create --generate-ssh-keys ...`.
`~/.ssh/id_rsa` is overwritten without warning.

**Expected behavior**
Show a warning when a private and/or public key exists and bail.


**Environment summary**
Install Method (e.g. pip, interactive script, apt-get, Docker, MSI, edge build) / CLI version (`az --version`) / OS version / Shell Type (e.g. bash, cmd.exe, Bash on Windows)  

**Additional context**

Further improvement would involve using different naming convention for the keypair used by the VM, or at least allow a different naming convention to be specified (possibly via `--ssh-key-value` since defaulting to `id_rsa[.pub]` can severely affect the a user's machine.

/cc @anth1y

issue_title: Exponential retry policy takes a parameter "increment_power" but seems to actually mean "increment_base"
issue_body:
For example:
When setting up a retry policy like so:
```
azure.storage.common.ExponentialRetry(
                initial_backoff=3,
                increment_power=2,
                max_attempts=3)
```
This _sounds_ like it will result in a retry sequence of: `{3, 3 + 1^2, 3 + 2^2}` (since `increment_power` is 2, the powers/exponents are all 2). In reality it generates a sequence `{3, 3 + 2^1, 3 + 2^2}`

Luckily, this is all clarified by the documentation here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-python/blob/9b5e870e615dca75cf9edcf7d68b2cc5f1a61e02/azure-storage-common/azure/storage/common/retry.py#L158

Still, it would be more clear if this were called `increment_base` rather than `increment_power` since the "power"/exponent is actually `n` (number of retries) and is not configurable by the user.

issue_title: canot set authorization header
issue_body:
## Long story short

<!-- Please describe your problem and why the fix is important. -->
Cannot set the Authorization header with this code.

## Steps to reproduce

```py
import asyncio
 
import aioec
 
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
client = aioec.Client(token='BNI=;6pXUYxftBItgvTAtN6X7bDGDluCnEwOnavKG9i3A4MY=', loop=loop)
 
print(loop.run_until_complete(client.login()))
 ```
That is a test token, and safe to reproduce. It has no access other than authorization.

## Expected behaviour

`1234` is outputted.

## Actual behaviour

`aioec.errors.LoginFailure: Invalid or incorrect token has been passed.` is outputted

## Reason

It seems that the `Authorization` header is not being set. printing `response.request_info.headers['Authorization']` inside `aioec/http.HttpClient.request` raises a `KeyError: "Key not found: 'Authorization'"`, even though the headers are clearly set in [aioec/http.py at 5ae0994 L47-L151](https://github.com/EmojiConnoisseur/aioec/blob/5ae09946c0c83ebf311f825b8358b9082d6e3bde/aioec/http.py#L47-L51) and I can confirm that self._session._default_headers['Authorization'] is set.

## Workaround

**Downgrade to aiohttp>=2.0.0,<2.3.0 and yarl<1.2.**
This is why I believe the issue to be aiohttp based and not in my code, unless I'm using aiohttp incorrectly.

## Your environment

issue_title: Cython and Python versions of CIMultiDict treat key case differently
issue_body:
First noticed this in 3.3.2 (probably existed before that), still exists in 4.0.0:

As one would expect (IMO) the Cython version uses the title-ized key for matching and preserves the original key for output/serialization. The Python version throws away the original key completely and uses the title-ized key for both purposes, breaking output. Again, that's my opinion, but in any case, the behavior should be the same.

```python
In [1]: from multidict._multidict import CIMultiDict  # Cython

In [2]: md = CIMultiDict()
   ...: md['some_key'] = 1
   ...: list(md.items())
   ...:
Out[2]: [('some_key', 1)]  # outputs original key

In [3]: from multidict._multidict_py import CIMultiDict  # Python

In [4]: md = CIMultiDict()
   ...: md['some_key'] = 1
   ...: list(md.items())
   ...:
Out[4]: [('Some_Key', 1)]  # outputs title-ized key
```

        if isinstance(resource, web.Resource):
            cors.add(resource)
    logger.info("Initialized CORS for {}".format(app['name']))
```

and i am loading _init_cors_ on _app.on_startup_

The issue seems to occur when i defer cors setup to on_startup signal like

```python
app.on_startup.append(init_cors)
```

But it works normally if i put the cors init code on app setup like
```python
 # register routes here
    register_routes(app)

    cors = aiohttp_cors.setup(app, defaults=CORS_DEFAULTS)

    # add resources to cors
    for resource in list(app.router.resources()):
        if isinstance(resource, web.Resource):
            cors.add(resource)
```

issue_title: Allow nbgrader apps to use -f and --force

Currently only --force is supported, which means you have to do:

nbgrader autograde ps1 --force

nbgrader autograde ps1 -f

Both should be supported flags.

issue_title: Why does MathJax render equations without `$$` in the notebook?
issue_body:
In the "textbooks with jupyter" project, I realized that there was an interesting difference of equation rendering behavior between jupyter notebooks UI and how Jekyll renders the markdown. Specifically, if I put a set of equations inside a markdown cell in Jupyter Notebooks, but without the `$$` delimiting a mathjax block, then it'll still be rendered. E.g., [from this page](https://www.inferentialthinking.com/chapters/14/1/Properties_of_the_Mean)
```
The mean of the collection {2, 3, 3, 9} is 4.25, which is not the "halfway point" of the data. So then what does the mean measure?

To see this, notice that the mean can be calculated in different ways.

\begin{align*}
\mbox{mean} ~ &=~ 4.25 \\ \\
&=~ \frac{2 + 3 + 3 + 9}{4} \\ \\
&=~ 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} ~~ + ~~ 3 \cdot \frac{1}{4} ~~ + ~~ 3 \cdot \frac{1}{4} ~~ + ~~ 9 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \\ \\
&=~ 2 \cdot \frac{1}{4} ~~ + ~~ 3 \cdot \frac{2}{4} ~~ + ~~ 9 \cdot \frac{1}{4} \\ \\
&=~ 2 \cdot 0.25 ~~ + ~~ 3 \cdot 0.5 ~~ + ~~ 9 \cdot 0.25
\end{align*}

The last expression is an example of a general fact: when we calculate the mean, each distinct value in the collection is *weighted* by the proportion of times it appears in the collection.
```

this will render fine in the notebook, but when Jekyll tries to render it, MathJax won't parse it unless you add `$$`.

I'm wondering if there's some kind of mathjax configuration that the notebook uses to "auto-detect" code blocks like this, or if the notebook app is doing some sanitizing of the markdown before rendering. Anybody have an idea on this? maybe @Carreau or @takluyver ?

issue_title: Enhancement: Add line break after The Jupyter Notebook is running at:
issue_body:
[I 13:47:40.278 NotebookApp] The Jupyter Notebook is running at: http://localhost:8888/?token=b5d714ae8bd101fd251053ddc55add86fafb8e0c1ddd690f
is displayed in the console on starting jupyter notebook and if the browser has not been set or cannot be found the user needs to copy and paste this into a browser.

The trouble is that the standard console or command window width is 80 characters while the message is 142 which, on many systems, results in the text wrapping which is not convenient for copy and paste. 

Splitting the message over 2 lines should enable the entire text that needs to be copied to appear on a single line.  If some other tools use the output format adding a backslash at the end of the first line should satisfy most if not all of them.

issue_title: [Feature Request] Provide a way to navigate to a cell by its index
issue_body:
The utility **`nbshow`** (part of `nbdime`) will show low-level content details of Jupyter/IPython notebooks. Cells are shown with their **index** (starting from 0). When hunting down and fixing low-level problems in notebooks, it would be convenient to have ---in the Jupyter Notebook Editor--- a way of navigating to a cell with a given index.

When there are code cells, and these code cells have been executed, you can use the `execution_count` (`In[#]` and `Out[#]`) as a navigation aid. But this is still an awkward substitute for direct navigation based on cell index.

issue_title: A better implementation of device placement?
issue_body:
It is known Sonnet module fails to merit the simple device placement directive, which was addressed as in issue #61 , there @kosklain offered a good solution. However there is a tiny problem in practice. Just look at the demonstrating code below:

```
import tensorflow as tf
import sonnet as snt

class RCNNOutput(snt.AbstractModule):
    def __init__(self, out_size, name = "rcnn_output"):
        super(RCNNOutput, self).__init__(name = name)
        with self._enter_variable_scope():
            bf = snt.BatchFlatten(name = "bf")
            fc0 = snt.Linear(output_size = 256, name = "fc0")
            fc1 = snt.Linear(output_size = out_size, name = "fc1")
            self._seq = snt.Sequential([bf, fc0, tf.nn.relu, fc1], name = "seq")

    def _build(self, inputs):
        return self._seq(inputs)

def test():
    import numpy as np
    from tensorflow.core.framework import node_def_pb2

    num_gpus = 2

    def get_device_setter(gpu_id):
        def device_setter(op):
            _variable_ops = ["Variable", "VariableV2", "VarHandleOp"]
            node_def = op if isinstance(op, node_def_pb2.NodeDef) else op.node_def
            return  '/cpu:0' if node_def.op in _variable_ops else '/gpu:%d' % gpu_id
        return device_setter

    with tf.device(get_device_setter(0)):
        rcnn_output = RCNNOutput(4)

    with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
        t = tf.constant(np.ones([8, 32, 32, 3]), np.float32)
        ts = tf.split(t, num_gpus)

    total_outputs = []
    for i in range(num_gpus):
        with tf.device(get_device_setter(i)):
            output = rcnn_output(ts[i])
            total_outputs.append(output)

    total_outputs = tf.add_n(total_outputs)

    writer = tf.summary.FileWriter("rcnn_output_output", tf.get_default_graph())

    config = tf.ConfigProto(log_device_placement = True)

    with tf.Session(config = config) as sess:
        sess.run([tf.global_variables_initializer(), tf.local_variables_initializer()])

        v = sess.run(total_outputs)
        print(v)

    writer.close()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    test()

```

The first concern is the following code snippet in the above code example:

```
    with tf.device(get_device_setter(0)):
        rcnn_output = RCNNOutput(4)
        t = tf.constant(np.ones([8, 32, 32, 3]), np.float32)

```
Here 0 is just for some placeholder purpose, since the intention is to have the model parameters placed on CPU, in order to be shared across multiple GPUs. Although I could define get_device_setter in some prototype like get_device_setter(gpu_id = 0), probably someone still makes the criticism that here the sole intention is to construct everything on CPU, why a GPU ID involves? 

The criticism is tended to be a little stronger when the code snippet below is added together:

```
    with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
        t = tf.constant(np.ones([8, 32, 32, 3]), np.float32)
        ts = tf.split(t, num_gpus)

```
The intention here is we have all inputs prepared on CPU. So people will say, why you mix directives together, why you can not place them under one directive? Simply answering because Sonnet doesn't support the simple device placement directive, so it is can not be done in a harmonic way, is probably not a good answer to ease every skepticism.

So I just wonder could it be done a little nicer than the above approach, namely use a more obvious directive for a neatly placement control both for inputs preparation and model construction?

Many thanks.

issue_title: Stash filestore token even when filled
issue_body:
I've ran into an issue with filtering the data.
Generally we want to filter on the actual data, which requires the events to be filled. After filtering it would be nice to have the option of passing the data into the databroker (for instance if your filtering took a long time to execute). However, the data didn't change (the image is still the same) only how we have represented it in MDS. In this situation it might be nice to have the filestore tokens handy, as we could bypass saving the data onto disk and just hand MDS the existing FS token. With this in mind would it be possible to stash the filestore token somewhere inside the event data even after filling?

issue_title: IDE specific auto-generated files need to be in gitignore.
issue_body:
In the recent times many IDEs have become popular for Python development. Among them are Jetbrain Community's [Pycharm](https://www.jetbrains.com/pycharm/) and [PyDev](http://www.pydev.org/), an IDE extended from Eclipse for Python development.

Upon opening a project within, these IDEs generate certain files and directories which are specific to them, and are not project specific.

These files and directories need not be pushed on Github with the main source. So they must be included in _.gitignore_ file.

issue_title: Hurry mode for customer alerts is never True
issue_body:
[The code](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/62014cf6e36f5e1e612adc47dcbd7f859db3be4b/src/oscar/apps/customer/alerts/utils.py#L68-L77) to determine if hurry mode is set for a customer alert is checking that the number of active alerts are _less_ than the total number of items in stock for a product. 

```
if num_stockrecords == 1:
    num_in_stock = stockrecords[0].num_in_stock
    # hurry_mode is false if num_in_stock is None
    hurry_mode = num_in_stock is not None and num_alerts < num_in_stock
else:
    result = stockrecords.aggregate(max_in_stock=Max('num_in_stock'))
    hurry_mode = result['max_in_stock'] is not None and \
        num_alerts < result['max_in_stock']
```

I think it should be the reverse - check if the number of active alerts for a product are _greater_ than the number in stock. I'll submit a pull request to fix this.

issue_title: running client on OS X fails with permission errors
issue_body:
It seems that there is some python code that tries to test if a port is open which does not work unless running under sudo. (This also means the integration tests always fail on OS X machines.)

I believe that is being done in order to see if the client can later open that port itself. A nice solution would be to just try to open the port and then handle the failures there, instead. This has the nice property of removing a possible race between the time of port-check and the time of port-allocation where another service could take the port.

issue_title: data validation error
How to create a Schema containing a dict of nested Schema?
issue_body:
Hi. I've been digging around and couldn't find the answer to this.

Say I've got a model like this:

```python
class AlbumSchema(Schema):
    year = fields.Int()

class ArtistSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str()
    albums = ...
```

I want `albums` to be a `dict` of `AlbumSchema`, so that `ArtistSchema` serializes as

```python
{ 'albums': { 'Hunky Dory': {'year': 1971},
              'The Man Who Sold the World': {'year': 1970}},
  'name': 'David Bowie'}
```

Naively, I would expect syntaxes like this to work:

```python
fields.List(Schema)
fields.Dict(Schema)
```
or maybe

```python
fields.List(fields.Nested(Schema))
fields.Dict(fields.Nested(Schema))
```

Serializing a `list` of `Schema` can be achieved through `Nested(Schema, many=True)`, which I find less intuitive, and I don't know about a `dict` of `Schema`.

Is there any way to do it? Or a good reason *not* to do it?

(Question also [asked on SO](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/38048775/marshmallow-dict-of-nested-schema).)

issue_title: Cannot use Tensor or AbstractModule as key in HashMap or HashSet
issue_body:
Currently, the `hashCode()` method in `Tensor` and `AbstractModule` is implemented using their own mutable states. 
If we put an `AbstractModule`  in a HashSet, and call forward on `AbstractModule`, then we may not find
the `AbstractModule` in the HashSet it was put in.

 For example, the following test will fail:
```scala
  "HashSet " should "work" in {
    val layer1 = Linear(4, 4)
    val hashSet = mutable.HashSet[Module[Float]]()
    hashSet.add(layer1)
    val data = Tensor[Float](4, 4).rand()
    layer1.forward(data)
    hashSet.contains(layer1) should be (true)
  }
```

issue_title: Crash on using floating point and real transformation (version Win-64 bits 4.4.0)
issue_body:
The following smt2 code makes the last stable binary release (4.4.0) crash on windows 64 bits.

	(declare-const x Real)
	(declare-const y Real)
	(declare-const res Real)

	(define-fun NonLinProd( (num1 Real) (num2 Real) (result Real) ) Bool
		(and
			(fp.eq 
				((_ to_fp 11 53) roundTowardZero result) 
				(fp.mul roundTowardZero 
					((_ to_fp 11 53) roundTowardZero num1) 
					((_ to_fp 11 53) roundTowardZero num2)))
		)	
	)
	;(assert (= res 25))
	(assert (NonLinProd x y res))
	(check-sat)
	;(get-model)

Actually, if result and res are floating point numbers of the good type, it also crashes. If everything (num1, num2, x, y) is floating point, then it works fine.

While I am here, I might as well ask some question that are related to this piece of code. As you can see, I was playing with the translation between reals and floating point. 

* In the API documentation and in some StackOverflow answers, the following warning is mentioned concerning the function “to_real”. However, there is no similar warning for the function “to_fp”. Is the warning also true for “to_fp”?

>  Note that this type of conversion will often result in non-linear constraints over real terms.

* Depending on your answer from the first question, wouldn’t the code shown here be able to mix together reals and floating points without non-linear constraints?

* Does z3 support a fixed point precision numbers as opposed to floating point numbers or do you have any plans to introduce them? The exponent being a constant in this case would probably eliminate non-linear constraints. This kind of precision would be “good enough” for my needs and probably for many problems as well.

issue_title: feature request: platform independent step limit
issue_body:
Hi,

We notice the step limit (using memory_max_alloc_count=BLAH) seems to be platform dependent. In particular we have a VC that solves on OSX with a given step-limit, but not on 64-bit Linux.

Limiting the number of allocations always struck me as slight low-level, are there any plans to have something more platform independent like CVC4's --rlimit feature? It doesn't need to be the same for two different revisions of Z3; but ideally its the same no matter the architecture.

The VC in question can be easily provided if you're interested.

Thanks,

   Florian

issue_title: Z3 loops and ignores soft time-out
issue_body:
In the attached file, the variables `s#0` and `t#0` are set up in the same way. Trying to prove something about `s#0` immediately returns with a result, whereas changing the file to try to prove the same thing for `t#0` causes Z3 (version 4.4.1 or 4.4.0) to immerse itself in its toil to the extent that it even ignores the soft timeout.

To repro:
* See the end of the attached input file.  Choose either the marked `s#0` line or the marked `t#0` line.  Then run z3.exe version 4.4.1 or 4.4.0 on the input file.
[dd.smt2](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/37424/dd.smt2.txt)

Notes:
* I am, of course, aware of that minute changes in the input can cause dramatic changes in Z3's behavior. However, the fact that the soft timeout gets ignored here smells to me like some bug.
* I have distilled this file from a larger one with more axioms, where the `s#0` line resulted in a Valid (that is, unsat) query. In that case, using the `t#0` line caused Z3 never to come back at all.  With the attached file, Z3 does terminate on the `t#0` line, but after about 25 seconds, even with `-t:3000`.  I'd be happy to furnish this larger input file, if you want it.

issue_title: Enum34 is not compatible with Python 3.6
issue_body:
We've been hitting a bug with PyFilesystem2 in conjunction with Python 3.6. Depending on how the Python path is set up we are unable to use the enum features introduced in 3.6 if we also install PyFilesystem2.

The problem is that [enum34 does not support Python 3.6](https://bitbucket.org/stoneleaf/enum34/issues/19/enum34-isnt-compatible-with-python-36) and should therefore not be installed if the version used is greater than Python 3.4.

This is what you can use to test it locally:

```bash
virtualenv -p python3.6 venv
venv/bin/pip install fs
PYTHONPATH='./venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages' venv/bin/python -c "from enum import IntFlag"
```

Can this be changed? An option might be to use [enum-compat](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/enum-compat) which installs enum34 conditionally. I see there's also some sort of version-specific dependency in your setup.py though I haven't ever encountered that before.

issue_title: [jext] Directory Listing Styling
issue_body:
Some feedback I've gotten from folks about the directory listing, that I agree with:

* Make the font of the directory listing smaller than it currently is (pretty easy)
* People like the directory listing UI that is in commuter
  * I originally intended to use the same component, then realized I couldn't do a straight `mv` because of differences in routing (`Link` from next.js).

My general recommendation is to create a directory listing package as part of the monorepo, creating them as compound components like this:

```jsx
<DirectoryListing>
  <Entry>
    <Entry.Icon type="notebook" />
    <Entry.Name>
      <a href="..." /> 
    </Entry.Name>
    <Entry.Time>
      <TimeAgo date={row.last_modified} />
    </Entry.Time>
  </Entry>
</DirectoryListing>
```

issue_title: problems in rs_convert
issue_body:
| Required Info                         |                                                                |
|---------------------------------|------------------------------------------- |
| Camera Model                       | D400 | 
| Firmware Version                   | 05.09.09.02 | 
| Operating System & Version |   MacOS  | 
| Kernel Version (Linux Only)    |                                | 
| Platform                                 | Mac  |
| SDK Version                            |  2.11.0                          | 
| Language                            |  {C++ }                          | 

I tested the recently released version of the rs_convert tool. However I found the following problems:

1. Even with small bag files (~150MB), it drops frames. I was unable to extract all frames from a bag with 427 color and 430 depth frames. The number of frames dropped varies in each execution instance.

2. Sometimes it crashes before completion. I suspect it is also because of timing issues. When debugging, it points to the following line:

```
void Writer::initializeLogger(const std::string& loggerId, bool lookup, bool needLock) {
  if (lookup) {
    m_logger = ELPP->registeredLoggers()->get(loggerId, ELPP->hasFlag(LoggingFlag::CreateLoggerAutomatically));
  }
```

3. It does not seem to have a smooth execution. Sometimes it gets stuck at a certain completion percentage and then it gets a burst of speed.



I was hoping to use the tool as a base for non-realtime processing of rosbags frame-by-frame. However, every time I tried with playback.resume() and playback.pause() even before the rs_convert tool was released I ran in issues 1 or 2. Are there any suggestions for my use-case?

issue_title: A2C Loss Function Value vs Policy
issue_body:
The A2C loss function used in this repository is the following:
`        neglogpac = tf.nn.sparse_softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits=train_model.pi, labels=A)

        pg_loss = tf.reduce_mean(ADV * neglogpac)
        entropy = tf.reduce_mean(cat_entropy(train_model.pi))
        vf_loss = tf.reduce_mean(mse(tf.squeeze(train_model.vf), R))
        loss = pg_loss - entropy*ent_coef + vf_loss * vf_coef
Where there is one loss function for updating all weights in the NN (both value and policy function approximators).

Yet, both [Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction](http://incompleteideas.net/book/bookdraft2017nov5.pdf) and the original A3C paper Mnih [16'] [Asynchronous Methods For Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.01783.pdf) implement two loss functions, one for the value function and one for the policy function:

`w <- w + alpha*discount*advantage*value_gradient`
`theta <- theta + alpha*discount*advantage*log_policy_gradient`

Why does the A2C implementation here only utilize a combined loss function, whereas the literature utilize separate loss functions for the value and policy function approximators?

issue_title: Improve performance in Travis CI builds with greater parallelization
issue_body:
Currently, when building watchmaker using Travis CI, the `check` job must be successful before the `docs` job will start which must be successful before the 6 `test` jobs can start. These 8 jobs can all be run in parallel to improve performance. 

While it is clear that performance will improve by parallelizing the jobs in the case where all 8 jobs will be successful, performance is also likely to improve in situations where 1+ job(s) fail.  Here any failure of any of the first 4-8 parallel jobs that Travis CI allocates will cause the entire build to fail. Testing more, sooner, leads to quicker discovery of errors. Further, the `check` and `docs` stages seem to rarely produce errors and thus are poorly suited as gatekeepers but function instead as bottlenecks.

Chages would be as follows:
- Add otCoapHeaderGetFirstOption - this allows for the application to do it's processing (e.g. query options).

issue_title: Spinel: Add Source Match Radio Functionality
issue_body:
With recent changes we have added the `otLinkRaw` API and hooked most of it up to NCP and exposed it through Spinel properties. The only APIs left are related to the Source Match functionality:

```
otLinkRawSrcMatchEnable
otLinkRawSrcMatchAddShortEntry
otLinkRawSrcMatchAddExtEntry
otLinkRawSrcMatchClearShortEntry
otLinkRawSrcMatchClearExtEntry
otLinkRawSrcMatchClearShortEntries
otLinkRawSrcMatchClearExtEntries
```

I was thinking we could add 3 new properties for this functionality:

`SPINEL_PROP_MAC_SRC_MATCH_ENABLED` to support enabling/disabling, via  the `SPINEL_CMD_PROP_VALUE_SET` command.

`SPINEL_PROP_MAC_SRC_MATCH_SHORT_ADDRESSES` to support operations on the short address match list. It would support `SPINEL_CMD_PROP_VALUE_SET`, `SPINEL_CMD_PROP_VALUE_INSERT` and `SPINEL_CMD_PROP_VALUE_REMOVE`.

`SPINEL_PROP_MAC_SRC_MATCH_EXTENDED_ADDRESSES` to support operations on the extended address match list. It would function just as the short address property.

One thing to note, as there are no APIs for querying, we wouldn't be able to support `SPINEL_CMD_PROP_VALUE_GET` for any of these properties, but there is also no requirements to support a get.

Any comments?

issue_title: can't convert bp::object()
issue_body:
In the past (boost::python) I've used bp::object() for defaulted arguments e.g.,

```
    .def ("__call__", &wrapped_pn<int_t>::generate,
          //bp::default_call_policies(),
          py::arg ("size"),
           py::arg ("dtype")=object()
          )
```

But with pybind11 this gives:
```
ImportError: arg(): could not convert default keyword argument 'dtype: boost::python::api::object' in method '<class 'pn2.pn64'>.__call__' into a Python object (type not registered yet?)
```

Is there either a fix for this, or another recommended way to indicate defaulted arguments?

Syncing account on dav.openxchange.eu
issue_body:
Hello, did anyone ever sync an account on openxchange.eu successfully? I ran into the following issue:

```
error: The server returned something vdirsyncer doesn't understand. Error message: InvalidXMLResponse('Invalid XML encountered: mismatched tag: line 1, column 2777\nDouble-check the URLs in your config.',)
error: While this is most likely a serverside problem, the vdirsyncer devs are generally interested in such bugs. Please report it in the issue tracker at https://github.com/pimutils/vdirsyncer/issues
error: 1 out of 1 tasks failed.
```

I have similar problems with other Cal/CardDAV clients as well. Each of these not managing to find the avilable address books and/or calendars. The only working client I found is OX Sync [1] that offers a distinct selection of providers. This selection includes EuroDNS which is behind openxchange.eu.

OX Sync uses dmfs [2] which manages to connect to `https://dav.openxchange.eu` successfully. So I started to reverse engineer it a bit, trying to find out which URLs it attempts to connect. Unfortunately, I could not find out.

Before spending more time on this (taking into account my lack of knowledge about Cal/CardDAV): Has anyone ever synced an account on dav.openxchange.eu successfully?

[1]: https://play.google.com/store/apps/details?id=com.openexchange.mobile.syncapp.enterprise
[2]: https://github.com/dmfs/jdav

issue_title: Does nfcpy always polls when waiting for a NFC tag?
issue_body:
We use the following code to wait for a NFC tag and we are unsure how nfcpy handles this underneath...
```
import nfc

def connected(tag):
    """ Print the NFC Tag information"""

clf = nfc.ContactlessFrontend('tty:AMA0:pn532')

timeOut = lambda: time.time() - started > (5 * 60) 
started = time.time()
nfcTag = clf.connect(rdwr={'on-connect': connected}, terminate=timeOut)

""" Do something with the tag here"""
```
We are concern that nfcpy always poll waiting for a tag, thus the power consumption on our device is not optional.

Any feedback will be really appreciated...

issue_title: BQ documentation error
issue_body:
In https://github.com/googledatalab/pydatalab/blob/master/google/datalab/bigquery/_table.py for 
class Table.__init__, the docs say the name can be in the form <project>:<dataset>.<table>, but it actually has to be in the form <project>.<dataset>.<table>

Same for the View class in https://github.com/googledatalab/pydatalab/blob/master/google/datalab/bigquery/_view.py

issue_title: Separate development requirements from the application requirement
issue_body:
Development dependencies:

```
pytest
pytest-timeout
sphinx
```

These can be moved to a `requirements-dev.txt` file:

```
-r requirements.txt

pytest
pytest-timeout
sphinx
```

Or we can remove the magic from the `setup.py` and leave `requirements.txt` as is [python-guide](http://docs.python-guide.org/en/latest/writing/structure/?highlight=requirements#requirements-file).

issue_title: Standard stdout and stderr for PBS jobs
issue_body:
When a PBS job failed, it is natural to report stdout/stderr to users. However, the location of these files are usually set by users in the job script and not known to SoS. Perhaps we should

1. Force the use of `.sos/tasks/${task_id}.out` and `.sos/tasks/${task_id}.err`. Basically sos would read from these two files and will report nothing if users specifies anything else in their job file.

2. Make these configurable, namely set something like the following

   ```
    stdout: `${proj_dir}/${task}.out`
    stderr: `${proj_dir}/${task}.err`
    job_template: |
        #PBS -o ${stdout}
        #PBS -e ${stderr}
        #PBS other options
    ```
    so that sos knows what is set by the job template.

This bug can be reproduced by doing the following.

```python
import ray
import numpy as np

@ray.remote
def f(x):
  print([x])
  return x

ray.get(f.remote(np.int64(3)))  # This prints ['\x03\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'] and returns '\x03\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00\x00'.
```

Note that the following code works.

```python
ray.get(ray.put(np.int64(3)))  # prints 3
```

issue_title: How to know when an actor is available.
issue_body:
Suppose we create a handful of actors, assign a task to each, and want to assign a new task to an actor whenever that actor becomes available. `ray.wait` allows us to know when an "object" is available, but what we really want is an easy mapping from the object ID to the actor object.

Some options:

1. If the actors are stored in a list, then pass the actor's index in the list into the actor constructor and return it from the relevant method.
2. Maintain a dictionary mapping object ID to actor.

Note that in a lot of these situations, the actors are considered interchangeable for the purposes of the application, so this might be addressed by creating "stateless" actors whose identities don't matter and where multiple replicas of the actor are hidden behind a single object that we call methods on.

issue_title: UnicodeDecodeError
issue_body:
I am using python 2.7.10. When running the following code I get a unicodedecodeerror

This is the code:

`
from pdfminer.pdfinterp import PDFResourceManager, PDFPageInterpreter
from pdfminer.converter import TextConverter
from pdfminer.layout import LAParams
from pdfminer.pdfpage import PDFPage
from StringIO import StringIO 

rsrcmgr = PDFResourceManager()
retstr = StringIO()
laparams = LAParams()
device = TextConverter(rsrcmgr, retstr, codec='utf-8', laparams=laparams)
fp = file('c:\users\public\data\pdfs\policy.pdf', 'rb')
interpreter = PDFPageInterpreter(rsrcmgr, device)
password = ""
maxpages = 1
caching = True
pagenos=set()

for page in PDFPage.get_pages(fp, pagenos, maxpages=maxpages,   password=password,caching=caching, check_extractable=True):
    interpreter.process_page(page)

text = retstr.getvalue()

fp.close()
device.close()
`



And this is the error:
`Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Users\Public\Public Software\WinPython32\python-2.7.10\lib\site-packages\spyderlib\widgets\externalshell\sitecustomize.py", line 790, in runfile
    execfile(filename, namespace)
  File "C:\Users\Public\Public Software\WinPython32\python-2.7.10\lib\site-packages\spyderlib\widgets\externalshell\sitecustomize.py", line 77, in execfile
    exec(compile(scripttext, filename, 'exec'), glob, loc)
  File "C:/Users/Public/Public Software/WinPython32/python-2.7.10/Scripts/pdfextract.py", line 28, in <module>
    text = retstr.getvalue()
  File "C:\Users\Public\Public Software\WinPython32\python-2.7.10\lib\StringIO.py", line 272, in getvalue
    self.buf += ''.join(self.buflist)
UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)`

After some digging, turns out the problem is that in PDFConverter the following thing happens:

`class PDFConverter(PDFLayoutAnalyzer):

    def __init__(self, rsrcmgr, outfp, codec='utf-8', pageno=1, laparams=None):
        PDFLayoutAnalyzer.__init__(self, rsrcmgr, pageno=pageno, laparams=laparams)
        self.outfp = outfp
        self.codec = codec
        if hasattr(self.outfp, 'mode'):
            if 'b' in self.outfp.mode:
                self.outfp_binary = True
            else:
                self.outfp_binary = False
        else:
            import io
            if isinstance(self.outfp, io.BytesIO):
                self.outfp_binary = True
            elif isinstance(self.outfp, io.StringIO):
                self.outfp_binary = False
            else:
                try:                    
                    self.outfp.write(u"é)  
                    self.outfp_binary = False
                except TypeError:
                    self.outfp_binary = True
        return`

As I am using StringIO from StringIO; the buflist in my StringIO object ends up with the u'é' entry which is unicode type. Later in the code when it is writing from the PDF to this array it writes str types. This mixing causes StringIO to throw a UnicodeDecodeError when it tries to join them all (in the getvalues() call).

I'm not that pro with Python, I managed to get it working by replacing the particular line by:

self.outfp.write(u"é".encode(codec,'ignore'))

But maybe this defeats the purpose of the line (?). 

I found a post on StackOverflow with some information that I thought was relevant:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/5701372/what-caused-this-traceback

issue_title: Inconsistent keys in Slider classes
issue_body:
The table below shows all of the keys (except `['layout', 'style', 'msg_throttle']` which are common to all widgets) for the `Slider` widget classes. To be explicit, the list of keys was generated by the `.keys` property of an instance of each class. 

Most of it is not surprising (e.g. no min/max for `SelectionSlider`) but a few things were:

+ There is a `readout_format` for all the numerical sliders except `IntRangeSlider`,
+ There is no `value` key for `SelectionSlider` or `SelectionRangeSlider`.
+ `options` is not a key for `SelectionSlider` or `SelectionRangeSlider`.

Both `value` and `options` are *attributes* of  `SelectionSlider` and `SelectionRangeSlider`. It may not be that important that they are not keys but wanted to point it out.

issue_title: Failure to install distribute/setuptools into virtualenv from a 'fresh' win32 python 2.7 build because of wrong directory structure
issue_body:
On windows, to freshly build Python 2.7:

    wget http://www.python.org/ftp/python/2.7.2/Python-2.7.2.tar.bz2
    tar -xf Python-2.7.2
    //Make sure you're in the VS2008 build environ
    cd Python-2.7.2\PCBuild
    call build.bat
    call build.bat -d

At this point, the *.pyd extension modules live next to python.exe (for example, _socket.pyd).

After installing distribute and virtualenv from this python.exe and then trying to create a virtualenv, I get this traceback:

    New python executable in env\Scripts\python.exe
    Installing distribute..............
      Complete output from command Z:\pyhack\env\Scripts\python.exe -c "#!python
    \"\"\"Bootstra... main(sys.argv[1:])
    " --always-copy -U distribute:
      Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<string>", line 485, in <module>
      File "<string>", line 480, in main
      File "<string>", line 185, in download_setuptools
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\Lib\urllib2.py", line 94, in <module>
        import httplib
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\Lib\httplib.py", line 71, in <module>
        import socket
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\Lib\socket.py", line 47, in <module>
        import _socket
    ImportError: No module named _socket
    ----------------------------------------
    ...Installing distribute...done.
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\scripts\virtualenv-script.py", line 9, in <module>
        load_entry_point('virtualenv==1.6.1', 'console_scripts', 'virtualenv')()
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 795, in main
        never_download=options.never_download)
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 892, in create_environment
        search_dirs=search_dirs, never_download=never_download)
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 593, in install_distribute
        search_dirs=search_dirs, never_download=never_download)
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 562, in _install_req
       cwd=cwd)
      File "Z:\pyhack\python\lib\site-packages\virtualenv.py", line 863, in call_subprocess
        % (cmd_desc, proc.returncode))
    OSError: Command Z:\pyhack\env\Scripts\python.exe -c "#!python
    \"\"\"Bootstra... main(sys.argv[1:])
    " --always-copy -U distribute failed with error code 1
    

Apparently, the creation of the virtualenv went OK, but it could not install distribute because it distribute's install script (eventually) can't import _socket.

Basically, if we're on win32, python assumes a standard layout for the existence of the *pyd extensions. As this is a python source build, this is not the case - the *pyd files live next to python.exe. The resulting interpreter does not place it's old path on the resulting sys,path, which eventually leads to an ImportError when trying to load a .pyd
